questions.question: What does mutation testing primarily evaluate?
options: Test suite sensitivity to behavioral changes
questions.explanation: Mutation testing checks whether tests fail when code is intentionally changed in small ways.

questions.question: What is a 'mutant' in mutation testing?
options: A copy of the program with a small syntactic change
questions.explanation: A mutant is a copy of the original program with one small change (e.g., replacing + with -, changing > to >=). If your tests still pass on the mutant, either the change is equivalent or your tests are weak.

questions.question: A high coverage suite has low mutation score. Most likely interpretation?
options: Assertions are weak or miss edge semantics
questions.explanation: Coverage can be high while assertions remain shallow; mutation testing exposes this mismatch.

questions.question: Why should teams review surviving mutants manually?
options: Some survivors reflect missing tests while others may be equivalent
questions.explanation: Survivors are not uniformly actionable; manual triage separates true gaps from equivalent cases.

questions.question: What is the mutation score, and what does a score of 100% mean?
options: 100% means all mutants were killed by tests — every change to the code caused at least one test to fail
questions.explanation: Mutation score = killed mutants / total mutants. A score of 100% means every mutant (small code change) was detected by at least one test. This is stronger than line or branch coverage — it measures whether tests actually verify behavior, not just execute code.
